title: Learn Pharmacokinetics
text: Welcome to Learn Pharmacokinetics, an interactive online guide for learning the basic principals of pharmacokinetics.

title: Learn Pharmacokinetics
text: Welcome to Learn Pharmacokinetics, an interactive online guide for learning the basic principals of pharmacokinetics.

title: Learn Pharmacokinetics
text:
Chapters

Foundations

Introduction to Mathematical Modelling

title: Chapter 1: Introduction to mathematical modelling
text:
We are going to start our journey with a short discussion on empirical data vs. theoretical data. Empirical data is a measured truth. For example, your final grade in pharmacokinetics is an empirical measurement. Your grade in pharmacokinetics can depend on several factors; one of them could be how many chapters of this book you have read. If we were to poll 10 students on how many chapters of this book they have read and compared that to their final grade, we may get a plot that looks something like this:









This plot suggests that there may be a relationship between how many chapters you read and how well you do in the course. The points on this graph represent empirical data. If I want to predict someone’s grade if they read only 5 chapters, I can’t do that from the empirical data because no one read only 5 chapters. I would need to look at the trend in the plot and guess based on the information we have.
One strategy we could try is drawing a line through the data points and filling in the missing gaps. Use the sliders below and play around with the input until you feel like the line you’ve drawn fits the data well.
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 650

library(shiny)

points_df <- data.frame(
  chapters = c(1, 3, 6,7,2),
  grade = c(25,50,75,98,30 )
)

ui <- fluidPage(
  sliderInput("intercept", "Intercept", min = -2, max = 5, value = 0, step = 0.1),
  sliderInput("slope", "Slope", min = -2, max = 50, value = 1, step = 0.5),
  plotOutput("model_plot", height = "400px")
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  output$model_plot <- renderPlot({
    plot(
      points_df$chapters,
      points_df$grade,
      xlim = c(0, 10),
      ylim = c(0, 100),
      pch = 19,
      cex = 1,
      col = "#117A8B",
      xlab = "Chapters Read",
      ylab = "Final Grade (%)"
    )

    abline(
      a = input$intercept,
      b = input$slope,
      col = "#C2410C",
      lwd = 3
    )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)

title: MetaboMiNR: An Online Tool to Analyze Label-Free Proteomic Experiments With a Focus on Metabolism and Nuclear Receptors
text:
Michael F. Saikali, Carolyn L Cummins
Endocrinology, Volume 167, Issue 2, February 2026
https://doi.org/10.1210/endocr/bqaf190

title: MetaboMiNR: An Online Tool to Analyze Label-Free Proteomic Experiments With a Focus on Metabolism and Nuclear Receptors
text:
Abstract
Label-free quantification (LFQ) proteomics is growing in popularity and becoming increasingly more accessible to researchers, empowering them to compare proteome-wide changes between different treatment conditions. However, it remains difficult to leverage the full potential of LFQ data when the researcher has limited experience in proteomics and/or bioinformatics due to the complexity of data analysis. Here, we present MetaboMiNR, an easy-to-use web application for the analysis of LFQ data with a focus on metabolism and nuclear receptors (NRs). MetaboMiNR guides users through an intuitive process with clear instructions and minimal user input to conduct statistical analysis and produce publication ready plots. Users may input a MaxQuant-generated output file and perform standard global analysis and data quality control with the click of a button. The application offers 3 additional unique features: 1) Metabolism Miner extracts a user-selected Reactome pathway from the dataset, 2) Nuclear Receptor Miner extracts the target genes of a user-selected NR, and 3) Individual Plotter produces publication-ready bar plots for a selected protein. The utility of this application was demonstrated by analyzing a previously published dataset from mice treated with LDT409, a synthetic peroxisome proliferator–activated receptor agonist. MetaboMiNR can be accessed freely at [https://cumminslab.shinyapps.io/MetaboMiNR/].
